title: Denver: Trenton with Thin Air?
subtitle: Happy people dodging Lime scooters
contentHtml:
I knew what I would find in the East Colfax McDonald’s when a block away, while walking between Colorado’s Capitol building and the beautiful Basilica of the Immaculate Conception. I was walking in the middle of the road because it was six a.m., empty except for scattered orange safety cones, and the sidewalks dotted with people sleeping in blanket nests. Also, if I looked just right, I could glimpse the Rocky Mountains behind me and I wanted to see big snow-capped majestic mountains. That is what I wanted Denver to be, an idyllic gateway to the Rockies, not Trenton with thin air.

I’d intentionally booked a fancier hotel than I usually do, right in the thick of the downtown, hoping to see Denver’s better side, but I’d messed up and found myself in a hotel with the same name, but a few steps down the revitalization curve. I didn’t mind though, because I’d found a sweet brew-pub several blocks away with campfires, no tourists, and chatty employees, who made my first evening in Denver a reminder how friendly, and sanguine, people in the West are, a nice antidote to North Eastern cynicism.

The inside of the McDonald's was as I expected it to be — every table taken up by a broken person, each differentiated by sub-type. There were the all-nighters, sleeping off prior sins, the ranters, carrying on a dialogue with nobody, the twitchers and pacers, their bodies working off a stimulant, the schizophrenic flipping between calm and rage, and the physically broken, slumped into wheelchairs strung with colored plastic bags like a Christmas tree with lights, and then the actively-trying-to-not-doze-off-ers, at booths surrounded by duffel, garbage, and tote bags, milking their coffee, eyes vacant, except when a lime scooter passes by, because as I soon found out, that's the workhorse of Denver's drug delivery.

Keeping watch over it all was T, an empathetic security guard at the start of a fourteen-hour shift. T was unfailingly polite, knew everyone’s name, and only roused to action when someone did something egregious (shouting at an employee, curling up in a booth.) and even then he suggested more than demanded. He explained that light touch, a calculated grace, was because of his own complicated past, made more so when a knee was mangled in high school football (running back) and ended his dream of being in the Marines.
